title: Combinatorics of Singular Cardinals
speaker: Dima Sinapova
abstract: Ever since Cohen invented forcing and showed the independence of the continuum hypothesis (CH), the powerset function of infinite cardinals has been a central theme in modern set theory. In this talk we will focus of the behavior of the powerset function of singular cardinals. A cardinal κ is singular if it can be written as the union of less than κ many sets, each of size less than κ. The singular cardinal hypothesis (SCH) is an analogue of CH for singular cardinals. While it is possible to violate it by forcing, such constructions are generally difficult to obtain and can be quite intricate. We will present some recent results on the relationship between violating SCH and the combinatorial properties of the singular cardinal.

proof_stages.label: SCH as the singular analogue of CH and its stronger constraints
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by defining SCH at a singular strong-limit cardinal and contrasting singular-cardinal exponentiation with regular-cardinal Easton/Cohen flexibility, emphasizing large-cardinal lower bounds and ZFC restrictions such as Silver-type first-failure phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Stationary reflection and the simultaneous-reflection SCH constraint
proof_stages.summary: Stationary reflection at d0d\kappa^+d0e is defined as a compactness principle, then put in tension with failure of SCH, and the Solovay-style theorem is stated that sufficiently much simultaneous reflection at the successor of a singular forces SCH.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness problem and the countable-cofinality consistency theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker asks whether the simultaneous-reflection obstruction is optimal and states the joint result that, from large cardinals, one can force failure of SCH at d0d\aleph_\omegad0e together with reflection, indeed finite simultaneous reflection, at d0d\aleph_{\omega+1}d0e.

proof_stages.label: Supercompact-limit reflection mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The standard reflection argument is recalled: from an d0d\omegad0e-sequence of supercompact cardinals with limit d0d\kappad0e, an elementary embedding with critical point some d0d\kappa_nd0e makes each relevant stationary set reflect at d0d\sup j``\kappa^+d0e, explaining why finite simultaneous reflection comes for free after suitable collapses.

proof_stages.label: Obstacle from ordinary Prikry singularization and the modified forcing strategy
proof_stages.summary: The original SCH-failure models fail reflection because Prikry/Magidor singularization leaves a stationary set of former-cofinality points nonreflecting, so the new construction instead uses Prikry-type products/overlapping extenders with interleaved collapses and an iteration killing nonreflecting stationary sets while preserving the needed Prikry structure.

proof_stages.label: Extension to uncountable cofinality
proof_stages.summary: The recent joint theorem is stated that, from d0d\omega_1d0e many supercompact cardinals, one can force failure of SCH at d0d\aleph_{\omega_1}d0e together with reflection at d0d\aleph_{\omega_1+1}d0e, while the general uncountable-cofinality and simultaneous-reflection strengthenings remain partly open.

proof_stages.label: Silver-motivated downward-transfer problems
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by Silver's theorem, the speaker asks whether failure of SCH plus reflection at d0d\aleph_{\omega_1+1}d0e must already imply the same conjunction at many lower d0d\aleph_{\eta+1}d0e, and discusses heuristics from Magidor/Prikry-style sequences and possible counter-forcings.

proof_stages.label: Tree-property compactness questions at successors of singulars
proof_stages.summary: The final part formulates open problems about combining stationary reflection with the tree property at successors of singulars, explains the embedding-and-pigeonhole narrowing obstruction caused by collapses and bad scales, and broadens the theme to whether successors of singulars can be made to behave like successors of regulars.

examples.name: Easton-style continuum flexibility at regular cardinals
examples.note: The speaker notes that, unlike singular-cardinal power sets, the continuum can be made d0d\aleph_\alphad0e for many countable d0d\alphad0e subject essentially only to monotonicity, illustrating the contrast motivating SCH combinatorics.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites Easton/Cohen flexibility for regular-cardinal continuum values as a contrast, without running any forcing or computation on it

examples.name: Prikry/Magidor SCH-failure model with a former-cofinality nonreflecting set
examples.note: The standard way of forcing SCH failure by singularizing a large cardinal leaves the stationary set of points of former cofinality d0d\kappad0e nonreflecting, and this obstruction visibly motivates the new strategy of not singularizing in that way and then killing nonreflecting stationary sets.

examples.name: Weak-square obstruction from Prikry-type singularization
examples.note: In answer to a question about incompatibilities, the speaker describes Prikry-type singularizations of an inaccessible into a successor of a singular that produce weak-square principles, illustrating a known obstruction to compactness principles such as the tree property.
